resume:
DANIEL OKAFOR
Staff Data Engineer | Seattle, WA | daniel.okafor@example.com | linkedin.com/in/dokafor

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Staff-level data engineer with eleven years of experience building and operating large-scale data platforms across e-commerce and fintech. I specialize in the unglamorous but load-bearing parts of data infrastructure: building reliable ingestion at scale, designing data models that survive five years of schema drift, owning the cost and reliability of a warehouse that hundreds of analysts depend on, and dragging organizations off of fragile cron-and-bash pipelines onto orchestrated, observable systems. I am at my best when a platform is on fire and nobody can tell why the numbers are wrong; I have a deep instinct for tracing a bad dashboard number back through six transformation layers to the one upstream producer that silently changed a field. I care more about correctness and trust in data than about adopting the newest tool, and I have repeatedly chosen boring, proven technology over hype when the stakes were high. I have led platform migrations affecting dozens of teams, mentored engineers from junior to senior, and set the technical direction for data quality and lineage standards adopted org-wide. I believe a data platform's real product is trust, and that every silent data error spends down that trust faster than any outage.

EXPERIENCE

NorthChannel Commerce — Staff Data Engineer (2020 to present)
Own the core data platform serving roughly 400 internal analysts, data scientists, and ML engineers across merchandising, finance, and operations. Tech stack: Snowflake, dbt, Airflow, Kafka, Spark, and a homegrown lineage service.
- Led the migration of the company's analytics warehouse from a self-managed Redshift cluster to Snowflake. The Redshift cluster had become a daily reliability problem: nightly transforms routinely overran into business hours, and concurrency scaling was eating budget. I designed the migration to be incremental and reversible, running both warehouses in parallel for three months with automated row-count and checksum reconciliation on the top 200 tables so no team had to take a leap of faith. We cut the nightly batch window from 7.5 hours to 2.1 hours and reduced warehouse spend by 28 percent year over year while doubling query concurrency.
- Designed and rolled out a column-level data-lineage and freshness system after a board-level revenue dashboard was wrong for nine days because an upstream service quietly renamed a field. The system parses dbt and Spark job metadata to build a directed graph from source tables to BI exposures, and emits a freshness SLA breach alert before analysts notice. Time-to-detection for upstream breakages dropped from a median of 2 days to under 20 minutes across the 30 highest-criticality datasets.
- Established the org's data-contract standard: producers declare schema and semantic guarantees, and a CI check blocks breaking changes from merging. Adoption went from zero to 70 percent of critical upstream producers in a year, and schema-drift incidents on contracted datasets fell to near zero.
- Mentor three mid-level engineers; two were promoted to senior under my guidance. Run the platform on-call rotation and author the postmortems for Sev1 and Sev2 data incidents.

Meridian Payments — Senior Data Engineer (2016 to 2020)
Built and operated ingestion and transformation for a payments-analytics platform handling reconciliation across multiple processors.
- Rebuilt the transaction-ingestion pipeline from a brittle set of cron-triggered Python scripts into an Airflow-orchestrated, idempotent pipeline with explicit retries and dead-letter handling. This eliminated a recurring class of duplicate-double-counting incidents that had twice caused finance to restate internal numbers.
- Designed the dimensional model for the settlement data mart used by the finance close process, which compressed the monthly close reconciliation from four days to one.
- Owned PII handling for the analytics layer: tokenization of card and account identifiers at ingestion, and column-level access controls so analysts never touched raw PII.

ByteCart (e-commerce startup) — Data Engineer (2013 to 2016)
- Stood up the company's first real data warehouse and ETL when reporting was being run off read replicas of the production database and slowing down the app.
- Built the first event-tracking pipeline (clickstream to warehouse) that the growth team used for funnel analysis.

TECHNICAL SKILLS
Warehouses and engines: Snowflake, Redshift, BigQuery, Spark, Trino.
Orchestration and transformation: Airflow, dbt, Dagster (familiar).
Streaming and ingestion: Kafka, Debezium CDC, Fivetran.
Languages: SQL (expert), Python (expert), Scala (working), Go (basic).
Reliability and observability: data-contract design, column-level lineage, freshness SLAs, anomaly detection on metrics.
Governance: PII tokenization, column-level access control, GDPR deletion workflows.

SELECTED IMPACT
- Cut a 7.5-hour nightly batch window to 2.1 hours and warehouse spend by 28 percent during the Snowflake migration.
- Reduced data-incident median time-to-detection from 2 days to under 20 minutes via lineage and freshness tooling.
- Drove data-contract adoption to 70 percent of critical producers, collapsing schema-drift incidents.

EDUCATION
B.S. in Computer Science, University of Washington (2009 to 2013).

WHAT I'M LOOKING FOR
A staff or principal data-engineering role where the core challenge is trustworthiness and scale of a data platform that real money or real decisions depend on, not a greenfield tool-shopping exercise. I want to own platform direction, set quality standards that outlive me, and keep mentoring engineers into owners.
jd: We're hiring a Staff Data Engineer to own the reliability, cost, and architecture of the data platform at a high-growth fintech. You will set technical direction for ingestion, transformation, and the warehouse that powers analytics and ML across the company; drive data-quality and lineage standards; lead platform migrations; and mentor senior and mid-level engineers. We expect deep experience operating a warehouse at scale (Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift), strong dbt and orchestration (Airflow/Dagster) chops, a track record of leading a major platform migration with minimal disruption, and demonstrated ownership of data correctness in an environment where wrong numbers have real financial consequences. You'll report to the Director of Data Platform and partner with analytics, ML, and finance. We especially value engineers who can trace a wrong number to its root cause and design systems that prevent silent data errors.

history.q: Give me the shape of your current platform at NorthChannel — who depends on it and what you own.
Walk me through the Redshift-to-Snowflake migration. Why migrate at all?
You ran both warehouses in parallel for three months. What exactly did you reconcile, and how?
On the top 200 tables — how did you pick those 200, and what did you do about the long tail?
Tell me about the nine-day wrong-revenue-dashboard incident. What actually happened?
Why did it take nine days to catch? What was missing?
Walk me through the lineage system you built in response — how does it know a dataset is stale?
You quote detection dropping from 2 days to under 20 minutes. How did you measure that?
Data contracts — what happens mechanically when a producer tries to merge a breaking change?
Give me one concrete, recent example: a wrong number you personally traced to root cause. Take me through it step by step.

candidate_last_answer: Yes — about two months ago. Situation: finance flagged that gross-merchandise-value in the executive dashboard was about 4 percent low for a single Tuesday, which on our volume is a few million dollars, so it mattered for the weekly business review. Task: I owned the platform and the on-call, so I took the investigation. Action: I started at the BI exposure and walked the lineage graph backward. The dbt model feeding GMV looked fine — row counts normal, tests green. So I went one layer up to the order-events fact table and compared that Tuesday's hourly event counts against the trailing four Tuesdays; one hour, 2 to 3 AM, was missing roughly 40 percent of its rows. That pointed at ingestion, so I checked the Kafka consumer lag and the Debezium CDC connector for the orders database, and found the connector had restarted during a database failover at 2:11 AM and resumed from a slightly stale offset, silently dropping a window of change events. Result: I backfilled the missing CDC window from the database binlog within about three hours, GMV corrected to the right figure before the Monday review, and the durable fix was adding a per-hour row-count reconciliation between the source DB and the events table that now alerts when any hour deviates more than 5 percent — which would have caught this in 20 minutes instead of a finance analyst catching it a day later. I also filed the postmortem and added connector-restart offset validation to our CDC runbook.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: resumed from a slightly stale offset, silently dropping a window of change events

expected-yield: probing the deeper failure mechanism — why the CDC connector resumed from a stale rather than committed offset on failover, whether offsets were committed at-least-once or at-most-once, and why the original design allowed silent gap loss, beyond the row-count reconciliation he bolted on afterward
